# Extended Case References — Lecture 2-PLy8soKXo

*Brief mentions of canonical cases that did not warrant a full `case_index.md` entry. Generated by reconciliation pass against the 1,676-cluster canon. Substantive treatments are in `case_index.md`.*

## Cases mentioned in passing

### Helicopter swashplate bearing failure
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "Helicopter swashplate bearing failure"
- **Anchor:** `§3.p2`
- **Quote (layer 3):** "One of the Japanese Navy helicopters crashed in the Sea of Japan, about five or ten thousand feet of water. Took a long time to get it up. The swashplate just seized up — the bearings seized up. It was down in the ocean for so long that by the time they got it up it was so corroded they couldn't quite tell. It was the first time anything like this had happened, even though this type of helicopter was first designed in 1964 or '71. They hadn't had failures until this one in the late '80s."

### Helicopter swashplate bearing failure
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "Helicopter swashplate bearing failure"
- **Anchor:** `§4.p1`
- **Quote (layer 3):** "*[Tom shows a photograph of the swashplate assembly.]* Here's the swashplate. It's a very complex assembly. This is one of the linkages, there's another linkage. When the swashplate tilts — this is either collective or cyclic — there's a bearing inside here. The bearing failed, and it failed on an aircraft that was on its initial two-hour flight. It had just been built, it hadn't been turned over. There was no DD-251 form — that's the standard form that transfers ownership from the contractor to the government. A one-page form, so you have a point in time of who owns that helicopter."

### Helicopter bearing failure and crash (Stratford)
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "Helicopter bearing failure and crash (Stratford)"
- **Anchor:** `§4.p2`
- **Quote (layer 3):** "One of the performance specifications was that every helicopter has to fly in a hover for two hours before you can sell it to the government. So they had these two test pilots doing their two-hour hover above the field. They weren't going anywhere, just hovering for a couple hours. Then it crashes, the guys are dead. They look at it afterward — the swashplate bearing seized up."

### Helicopter swashplate bearing failure
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "Helicopter swashplate bearing failure"

- **Quote (layer 3):** "One of the performance specifications was that every helicopter has to fly in a hover for two hours before you can sell it to the government. ### Helicopter swashplate bearing failure
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "Helicopter swashplate bearing failure"
- **Anchor:** `§5.p2`
- **Quote (layer 3):** "They've made thousands of these for lots of applications, and this was the largest one anyone in the world made — they were a sole source. There's a company in Germany that makes something similar, but not exactly like this. The prior history: yes, they'd had a failure of the swashplate on a Japanese helicopter. This was the second failure they had ever had, and they'd been producing this helicopter for twenty-five, thirty years. So this was sort of a new type of failure, and they didn't even know it was related to the Japanese failure until they started their board of inquiry. They said,..."

### Helicopter bearing precision manufacturing
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "Helicopter bearing precision manufacturing"
- **Anchor:** `§5.p6`
- **Quote (layer 3):** "That bearing is pressed into an aluminum housing, which is the upper swashplate. That aluminum housing is milled on a vertical boring mill. The thing that gives the shape to the bearing is the swashplate aluminum housing. The helicopter manufacturer manufactured that big aluminum housing themselves, and they just never bothered to look into the shape of that housing — and in fact they destroyed it. Not only that, about six months after this whole inquiry started, they destroyed the three-year-old milling machine that had made it. There was no way to go back and prove what happened to this..."

### Helicopter swashplate bearing failure
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "Helicopter swashplate bearing failure"

- **Quote (layer 3):** "That bearing is pressed into an aluminum housing, which is the upper swashplate. That aluminum housing is milled on a vertical boring mill. The thing that gives the shape to the bearing is the swashplate aluminum housing. The helicopter manufacturer manufactured that big aluminum housing themselves, and they just never bothered to look into the shape of that housing — and in fact they destroyed it. Not only that, about six months after this whole inquiry started, they destroyed the three-year-old milling machine that had made it. There was no way to go back and prove what happened to this..."

### Helicopter swashplate bearing failure
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "Helicopter swashplate bearing failure"
- **Anchor:** `§7.p1`
- **Quote (layer 3):** "Until this one went down at the helicopter company and killed two test pilots. Now the helicopter company says, "Oh, we have one like this in Japan." And the Navy down at Cherry Point has a pilot with one of these helicopters coming in for a landing. As he's about two feet off the ground, his swashplate seizes and he slams two feet down. Nothing happened, but it was a seizure. They take it apart and say, "We had a bearing failure." And all of a sudden within the next few months they had four or five bearing failures, and they grounded the whole fleet."

### Navy high-strength low-alloy steel carrier study
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "Navy high-strength low-alloy steel carrier study"
- **Anchor:** `§7.p4`
- **Quote (layer 3):** "I talked to some of the guys in the Navy who were charged with going down to Jacksonville and fixing this. They were getting phone calls at 2 a.m. at home saying, "A helicopter will land on your lawn at 3 o'clock, be on it." They were being ferried from home to Jacksonville, Florida, to start working on fixing this aircraft carrier. When the military needs to move, they can. That's a true story.

## §8. The FBI takeover at Kaydon and a perfect bearing that still failed [29:39]"

### Helicopter swashplate bearing failure
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "Helicopter swashplate bearing failure"
- **Anchor:** `§10.p6`
- **Quote (layer 3):** "We said, "Well, we want to see the milling machine that produced these swashplates." "Oh, that was three years old, it was too old, we scrapped it three weeks ago." A three-year-old, quarter-million-dollar milling machine, and they scrapped it. You don't think the helicopter company knows what happened? They knew exactly what happened. They had a milling machine that was out of spec, machined a potato-chipped swashplate. The Reali-Slim bearings took the shape of the swashplate. They did their own investigation. The fox was watching the hen house."

### Helicopter bearing precision manufacturing
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "Helicopter bearing precision manufacturing"
- **Anchor:** `§11.p1`
- **Quote (layer 3):** "This little story has a number of parts about precision measurement, but mostly it's one about how the system works. Ordinarily, if this had been a military helicopter, there would have been some Navy captain or Marine major in charge of the investigation, and they would have found the problem, because they would have invited the bearing manufacturer to participate. Someone would have said, "Oh, what about the swashplate?" Whenever those things came up — and they probably did come up at the meetings of the board of inquiry — that's when they went off the record."

### Helicopter swashplate bearing failure

Someone would have said, "Oh, what about the swashplate?" Whenever those things came up — and they probably did come up at the meetings of the board of inquiry — that's when they went off the record."
